title: McIlroy's Double Delight: Defends Masters Crown
excerpt: Rory McIlroy wins back-to-back Masters titles, holding off Scottie Scheffler with clutch play down the stretch to claim his sixth major championship.
body:
McIlroy's Masters Mastery

Rory McIlroy etched his name into Masters folklore on Sunday, capturing his second consecutive Green Jacket with a one-shot victory over Scottie Scheffler at Augusta National. The defending champion's 12-under 276 proved enough to hold off a charging field of the world's best, with McIlroy joining an exclusive club of just four men to win back-to-back Masters titles — Tiger Woods, Jack Nicklaus and Nick Faldo the others.

In what amounted to a masterclass in pressure golf, McIlroy held the lead or shared it after every round, his composure never wavering despite late challenges from Scheffler, Justin Rose and Cameron Young.

Winning Score
-12
Margin of Victory
1 shot
Major Championship #
6
Winner's Prize
$4.5m

Amen Corner Brilliance

The pivotal moment came at the Golden Bell, the 12th hole at Augusta National. With the lead up for grabs, McIlroy struck a perfect 9-iron into the heart of the green, settling inside seven feet. He buried the putt for birdie to move two clear — a shot that would ultimately prove the difference. Rose, chasing hard all afternoon, found water at the same hole and dropped shots he could not recover from.

From there, McIlroy's scrambling was the stuff of legend — a left-handed par save using just his putter behind the 15th green, miraculous escapes from the depths of Augusta's rough, and ice-cold putting when it mattered most. He finished with a final-round 71 to hold Scheffler, who carded a bogey-free 68 and briefly threatened before falling one short.

A Crowded Podium

The chasing pack made for compelling theatre. Justin Rose, looking for what would have been the feel-good story of the century at 45 years old, finished T3 at 10-under. Cameron Young and Tyrrell Hatton joined him on 10-under, along with Russell Henley, who played magnificently to climb the leaderboard down the stretch with four consecutive birdies.

What's Next

McIlroy's victory adds his sixth major championship and puts him in very real conversation about where his legacy stands in the modern era. At just 36, he has time and form on his side. The PGA Tour moves immediately to the RBC Heritage in South Carolina, but McIlroy's thoughts are surely already turning to the U.S. Open at Pinehurst in June. Scheffler, despite the loss, remains the world No. 1 and a threat in every major to come. The 2026 season is only getting started.
